{{Short description|Shrine in Obama, Fukui}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Wakasahiko Jinja
若狭彦神社 | image = 101120 Wakasahiko-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan07s5.jpg | caption = Wakasahiko Jinja Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Fukui Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|27|57.9|N|135|46|42.5|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Hoori; Toyotama-hime | founded_by = | established = c.714 | date_destroyed = | location =Obama-shi, Fukui-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = October 10 (upper shrine)
March 10 (lower shrine) | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Wakasahiko Jinja'''|若狭彦神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Obama, Fukui|Obama]] in [[Fukui Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Wakasa Province]]. The main festivals of the shrine are held annually on October 10 and March 10.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is actually a twin shrine, consisting of the {{nihongo|'''Wakasahiko Shrine'''|若狭彦神社| Wakasahiko jinja}}, or "upper shrine", and the {{nihongo|'''Wakasahime Shrine'''|若狭姫神社| Wakasahime jinja}}, or "lower shrine". It is also sometimes referred to as the {{nihongo|'''Onyu Myōjin'''|若狭彦神社}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Wakasahiko Jinja are: * Upper shrine: {{nihongo|'''[[Hoori|Hikohohodemi no Mikoto]]'''| 彦火火出見尊|}}, the son of [[Ninigi]] and grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]] * Lower shrine: {{nihongo|'''[[Toyotama-hime]]'''| 豊玉姫命|}}, the daughter of the sea deity, [[Watatsumi]]. ==Overview== The shrine is located at the foot of Mt. Tadagadake in the southeast from the center of Obama city. Wakasahiko Shrine was formerly worshipped by seafarers, as Hoori is said to have obtained magical beads with which he could manipulate the tides while residing at [[Ryūgū-jō]]; however, today he is regarded as the god of ''[[tatami]]'' mats, and is now also worshiped by people involved in [[interior decoration]]. Wakasahime Shrine is said to have a spiritual power for easy delivery and childcare. Currently, most of the festivals are held at the lower shrine, Wakasahime Shrine, and the priesthood is also resident only at the lower shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} ==History== The origins of Wakasahiko Jinja are unknown. According to the shrine's legend, the two ''kami'' appeared in the guise of people from [[Tang dynasty|Tang]] at Shiraishi hamlet in Shimonegori village, Onyu County and the Wakasahiko Jinja was built in 714. It was related to its present location in 715. The lower shrine, Wakasahime Jinja was built in 721. The shrine first appears in historical documentation in the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' in an entry dated 859, when the upper shrine was promoted to senior second Rank, and the lower shrine to junior third Rank. In the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records of 927, the shrine is listed as a ''Myōjin Taisha''. By the [[Kamakura period]], the Wakasahiko Jinja was named the ''ichinomiya'' and the Wakasahime Jinja as the ''ninomiya'' of the province. Originally the upper shrine was the center of rituals, but this shifted to the lower shrine in the [[Muromachi period]]. During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|national shrine, 2nd Rank|国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}}, under the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. == Precincts == === Wakasahiko Jinja (Upper shrine) === * [[Honden]] – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * [[Rōmon]] (Zuishin-mon) – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Gate – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property 101120 Wakasahiko-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan12s.jpg|Rōmon 101120 Wakasahiko-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan11s.jpg|Gate 若狭彦神社陰陽石.jpg|Yin-Yang stone === Wakasahime Jinja (Lower shrine) === * Honden – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Romon (Zuishin-mon) – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Gate – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Noh Stage * Shaso – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property 101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan01s5.jpg|Romon 101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan04s5.jpg|Gate 101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan06s5.jpg|Noh stage The Wakasahiko Jinja is located a 30-minute walk and the Wakasahime Jinja a ten-minute walk from [[Higashi-Obama Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Obama Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category-inline|Wakasa-Hiko-jinja}} {{commons Category-inline|Wakasa-Hime-jinja}} *[https://www.jinja-fukui.jp/detail/index.php?ID=20151027_171130 Fukui Jinja Honcho] *[https://www.fuku-e.com/010_spot/?id=570 Fukui Tourist Association site] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} ]] ]]{{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Wakasa Province]] [[Category:Obama, Fukui]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Fukui Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Obama City]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1]] [[Category:Wakasahiko Shrine]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[sn:Wakasahiko Shrine]]}} [[file:101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan07s5.jpg|right|thumb|260px|Wakasahime Jinja]] [[file:Wakasa-Hime-jinja Onyu Festival 001.jpg|right|thumb|260px|Onyu Festival at Wakasahime Jinja
(Festival dedicated to God)]] [[sn:Wakasahiko Shrine]] [[en:Wakasahiko Shrine]] [[sn:akasahiko Shrine]] [[sn: failed]]

{{Short description|Shrine in Obama, Fukui}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Wakasahiko Jinja
若狭彦神社 | image = 101120 Wakasahiko-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan07s5.jpg | caption = Wakasahiko Jinja Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Fukui Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|27|57.9|N|135|46|42.5|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Hoori; Toyotama-hime | founded_by = | established = c.714 | date_destroyed = | location =Obama-shi, Fukui-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = October 10 (upper shrine)
March 10 (lower shrine) | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Wakasahiko Jinja'''|若狭彦神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Obama, Fukui|Obama]] in [[Fukui Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Wakasa Province]]. The main festivals of the shrine are held annually on October 10 and March 10.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is actually a twin shrine, consisting of the {{nihongo|'''Wakasahiko Shrine'''|若狭彦神社| Wakasahiko jinja}}, or "upper shrine", and the {{nihongo|'''Wakasahime Shrine'''|若狭姫神社| Wakasahime jinja}}, or "lower shrine". It is also sometimes referred to as the {{nihongo|'''Onyu Myōjin'''|若狭彦神社}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Wakasahiko Jinja are: * Upper shrine: {{nihongo|'''[[Hoori|Hikohohodemi no Mikoto]]'''| 彦火火出見尊|}}, the son of [[Ninigi]] and grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]] * Lower shrine: {{nihongo|'''[[Toyotama-hime]]'''| 豊玉姫命|}}, the daughter of the sea deity, [[Watatsumi]]. ==Overview== The shrine is located at the foot of Mt. Tadagadake in the southeast from the center of Obama city. Wakasahiko Shrine was formerly worshipped by seafarers, as Hoori is said to have obtained magical beads with which he could manipulate the tides while residing at [[Ryūgū-jō]]; however, today he is regarded as the god of ''[[tatami]]'' mats, and is now also worshiped by people involved in [[interior decoration]]. Wakasahime Shrine is said to have a spiritual power for easy delivery and childcare. Currently, most of the festivals are held at the lower shrine, Wakasahime Shrine, and the priesthood is also resident only at the lower shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} ==History== The origins of Wakasahiko Jinja are unknown. According to the shrine's legend, the two ''kami'' appeared in the guise of people from [[Tang dynasty|Tang]] at Shiraishi hamlet in Shimonegori village, Onyu County and the Wakasahiko Jinja was built in 714. It was related to its present location in 715. The lower shrine, Wakasahime Jinja was built in 721. The shrine first appears in historical documentation in the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' in an entry dated 859, when the upper shrine was promoted to senior second Rank, and the lower shrine to junior third Rank. In the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records of 927, the shrine is listed as a ''Myōjin Taisha''. By the [[Kamakura period]], the Wakasahiko Jinja was named the ''ichinomiya'' and the Wakasahime Jinja as the ''ninomiya'' of the province. Originally the upper shrine was the center of rituals, but this shifted to the lower shrine in the [[Muromachi period]]. During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|national shrine, 2nd Rank|国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}}, under the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. == Precincts == === Wakasahiko Jinja (Upper shrine) === * [[Honden]] – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * [[Rōmon]] (Zuishin-mon) – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Gate – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property 101120 Wakasahiko-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan12s.jpg|Rōmon 101120 Wakasahiko-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan11s.jpg|Gate 若狭彦神社陰陽石.jpg|Yin-Yang stone === Wakasahime Jinja (Lower shrine) === * Honden – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Romon (Zuishin-mon) – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Gate – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Noh Stage * Shaso – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property 101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan01s5.jpg|Romon 101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan04s5.jpg|Gate 101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan06s5.jpg|Noh stage The Wakasahiko Jinja is located a 30-minute walk and the Wakasahime Jinja a ten-minute walk from [[Higashi-Obama Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Obama Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category-inline|Wakasa-Hiko-jinja}} {{commons Category-inline|Wakasa-Hime-jinja}} *[https://www.jinja-fukui.jp/detail/index.php?ID=20151027_171130 Fukui Jinja Honcho] *[https://www.fuku-e.com/010_spot/?id=570 Fukui Tourist Association site] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} ]] ]]{{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Wakasa Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Obama City]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1]] [[Category:Wakasahiko Shrine]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[sn:Wakasahiko Shrine]]}} [[file:101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan07s5.jpg|right|thumb|260px|Wakasahime Jinja]] [[file:Wakasa-Hime-jinja Onyu Festival 001.jpg|right|thumb|260px|Onyu Festival at Wakasahime Jinja
(Festival dedicated to God)]] [[sn:Wakasahiko Shrine]] [[en:Wakasahiko Shrine]] [[sn:akasahiko Shrine]] [[sn: failed]]

''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). revision.comment: Bot: append Wikidata-derived interwikis
revision.text:
{{Short description|Shrine in Obama, Fukui}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Wakasahiko Jinja
若狭彦神社 | image = 101120 Wakasahiko-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan07s5.jpg | caption = Wakasahiko Jinja Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Fukui Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|27|57.9|N|135|46|42.5|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Hoori; Toyotama-hime | founded_by = | established = c.714 | date_destroyed = | location =Obama-shi, Fukui-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = October 10 (upper shrine)
March 10 (lower shrine) | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Wakasahiko Jinja'''|若狭彦神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Obama, Fukui|Obama]] in [[Fukui Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Wakasa Province]]. The main festivals of the shrine are held annually on October 10 and March 10.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is actually a twin shrine, consisting of the {{nihongo|'''Wakasahiko Shrine'''|若狭彦神社| Wakasahiko jinja}}, or "upper shrine", and the {{nihongo|'''Wakasahime Shrine'''|若狭姫神社| Wakasahime jinja}}, or "lower shrine". It is also sometimes referred to as the {{nihongo|'''Onyu Myōjin'''|若狭彦神社}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Wakasahiko Jinja are: * Upper shrine: {{nihongo|'''[[Hoori|Hikohohodemi no Mikoto]]'''| 彦火火出見尊|}}, the son of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] and grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]] * Lower shrine: {{nihongo|'''[[Toyotama-hime]]'''| 豊玉姫命|}}, the daughter of the sea deity, [[Watatsumi]]. ==Overview== The shrine is located at the foot of Mt. Tadagadake in the southeast from the center of Obama city. Wakasahiko Shrine was formerly worshipped by seafarers, as Hoori is said to have obtained magical beads with which he could manipulate the tides while residing at [[Ryūgū-jō]]; however, today he is regarded as the god of ''[[tatami]]'' mats, and is now also worshiped by people involved in [[interior decoration]]. Wakasahime Shrine is said to have a spiritual power for easy delivery and childcare. Currently, most of the festivals are held at the lower shrine, Wakasahime Shrine, and the priesthood is also resident only at the lower shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} ==History== The origins of Wakasahiko Jinja are unknown. According to the shrine's legend, the two ''kami'' appeared in the guise of people from [[Tang dynasty|Tang]] at Shiraishi hamlet in Shimonegori village, Onyu County and the Wakasahiko Jinja was built in 714. It was related to its present location in 715. The lower shrine, Wakasahime Jinja was built in 721. The shrine first appears in historical documentation in the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' in an entry dated 859, when the upper shrine was promoted to senior second Rank, and the lower shrine to junior third Rank. In the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records of 927, the shrine is listed as a ''Myōjin Taisha''. By the [[Kamakura period]], the Wakasahiko Jinja was named the ''ichinomiya'' and the Wakasahime Jinja as the ''ninomiya'' of the province. Originally the upper shrine was the center of rituals, but this shifted to the lower shrine in the [[Muromachi period]]. During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|national shrine, 2nd Rank|国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}}, under the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. == Precincts == === Wakasahiko Jinja (Upper shrine) === * [[Honden]] – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * [[Rōmon]] (Zuishin-mon) – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Gate – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property 101120 Wakasahiko-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan12s.jpg|Rōmon 101120 Wakasahiko-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan11s.jpg|Gate 若狭彦神社陰陽石.jpg|Yin-Yang stone === Wakasahime Jinja (Lower shrine) === * Honden – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Romon (Zuishin-mon) – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Gate – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Noh Stage * Shaso – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property 101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan01s5.jpg|Romon 101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan04s5.jpg|Gate 101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan06s5.jpg|Noh stage The Wakasahiko Jinja is located a 30-minute walk and the Wakasahime Jinja a ten-minute walk from [[Higashi-Obama Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Obama Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category-inline|Wakasa-Hiko-jinja}} {{commons Category-inline|Wakasa-Hime-jinja}} *[https://www.jinja-fukui.jp/detail/index.php?ID=20151027_171130 Fukui Jinja Honcho] *[https://www.fuku-e.com/010_spot/?id=570 Fukui Tourist Association site] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} ]] ]]{{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Wakasa Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Obama City]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[sn:Wakasahiko Shrine]]}} [[file:101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan07s5.jpg|right|thumb|260px|Wakasahime Jinja]] [[file:Wakasa-Hime-jinja Onyu Festival 001.jpg|right|thumb|260px|Onyu Festival at Wakasahime Jinja
(Festival dedicated to God)]] [[sn:Wakasahiko Shrine]] [[en:Wakasahiko Shrine]] [[sn:akasahiko Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Wakasa-Hiko-jinja}} [[ja:若狭彦神社]] [[zh:若狹彥神社]]

{{Short description|Shrine in Obama, Fukui}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Wakasahiko Jinja
若狭彦神社 | image = 101120 Wakasahiko-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan07s5.jpg | caption = Wakasahiko Jinja Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Fukui Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|27|57.9|N|135|46|42.5|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Hoori; Toyotama-hime | founded_by = | established = c.714 | date_destroyed = | location =Obama-shi, Fukui-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = October 10 (upper shrine)
March 10 (lower shrine) | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Wakasahiko Jinja'''|若狭彦神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Obama, Fukui|Obama]] in [[Fukui Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Wakasa Province]]. The main festivals of the shrine are held annually on October 10 and March 10.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is actually a twin shrine, consisting of the {{nihongo|'''Wakasahiko Shrine'''|若狭彦神社| Wakasahiko jinja}}, or "upper shrine", and the {{nihongo|'''Wakasahime Shrine'''|若狭姫神社| Wakasahime jinja}}, or "lower shrine". It is also sometimes referred to as the {{nihongo|'''Onyu Myōjin'''|若狭彦神社}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Wakasahiko Jinja are: * Upper shrine: {{nihongo|'''[[Hoori|Hikohohodemi no Mikoto]]'''| 彦火火出見尊|}}, the son of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] and grandfather of [[Emperor Jimmu]] * Lower shrine: {{nihongo|'''[[Toyotama-hime]]'''| 豊玉姫命|}}, the daughter of the sea deity, [[Watatsumi]]. ==Overview== The shrine is located at the foot of Mt. Tadagadake in the southeast from the center of Obama city. Wakasahiko Shrine was formerly worshipped by seafarers, as Hoori is said to have obtained magical beads with which he could manipulate the tides while residing at [[Ryūgū-jō]]; however, today he is regarded as the god of ''[[tatami]]'' mats, and is now also worshiped by people involved in [[interior decoration]]. Wakasahime Shrine is said to have a spiritual power for easy delivery and childcare. Currently, most of the festivals are held at the lower shrine, Wakasahime Shrine, and the priesthood is also resident only at the lower shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} ==History== The origins of Wakasahiko Jinja are unknown. According to the shrine's legend, the two ''kami'' appeared in the guise of people from [[Tang dynasty|Tang]] at Shiraishi hamlet in Shimonegori village, Onyu County and the Wakasahiko Jinja was built in 714. It was related to its present location in 715. The lower shrine, Wakasahime Jinja was built in 721. The shrine first appears in historical documentation in the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' in an entry dated 859, when the upper shrine was promoted to senior second Rank, and the lower shrine to junior third Rank. In the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records of 927, the shrine is listed as a ''Myōjin Taisha''. By the [[Kamakura period]], the Wakasahiko Jinja was named the ''ichinomiya'' and the Wakasahime Jinja as the ''ninomiya'' of the province. Originally the upper shrine was the center of rituals, but this shifted to the lower shrine in the [[Muromachi period]]. During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|national shrine, 2nd Rank|国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}}, under the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. == Precincts == === Wakasahiko Jinja (Upper shrine) === * [[Honden]] – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * [[Rōmon]] (Zuishin-mon) – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Gate – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property 101120 Wakasahiko-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan12s.jpg|Rōmon 101120 Wakasahiko-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan11s.jpg|Gate 若狭彦神社陰陽石.jpg|Yin-Yang stone === Wakasahime Jinja (Lower shrine) === * Honden – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Romon (Zuishin-mon) – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Gate – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Noh Stage * Shaso – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property 101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan01s5.jpg|Romon 101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan04s5.jpg|Gate 101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan06s5.jpg|Noh stage The Wakasahiko Jinja is located a 30-minute walk and the Wakasahime Jinja a ten-minute walk from [[Higashi-Obama Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Obama Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category-inline|Wakasa-Hiko-jinja}} {{commons Category-inline|Wakasa-Hime-jinja}} *[https://www.jinja-fukui.jp/detail/index.php?ID=20151027_171130 Fukui Jinja Honcho] *[https://www.fuku-e.com/010_spot/?id=570 Fukui Tourist Association site] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} ]] ]]{{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Wakasa Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[sn:Wakasahiko Shrine]]}} [[file:101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan07s5.jpg|right|thumb|260px|Wakasahime Jinja]] [[file:Wakasa-Hime-jinja Onyu Festival 001.jpg|right|thumb|260px|Onyu Festival at Wakasahime Jinja
(Festival dedicated to God)]] [[sn:Wakasahiko Shrine]] [[en:Wakasahiko Shrine]] [[sn:akasahiko Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Wakasa-Hiko-jinja}} [[ja:若狭彦神社]] [[zh:若狹彥神社]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]]

''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. == Precincts == === Wakasahiko Jinja (Upper shrine) === * [[Honden]] – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * [[Rōmon]] (Zuishin-mon) – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Gate – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property 101120 Wakasahiko-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan12s.jpg|Rōmon 101120 Wakasahiko-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan11s.jpg|Gate 若狭彦神社陰陽石.jpg|Yin-Yang stone === Wakasahime Jinja (Lower shrine) === * Honden – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Romon (Zuishin-mon) – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Gate – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Noh Stage * Shaso – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property 101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan01s5.jpg|Romon 101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan04s5.jpg|Gate 101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan06s5.jpg|Noh stage The Wakasahiko Jinja is located a 30-minute walk and the Wakasahime Jinja a ten-minute walk from [[Higashi-Obama Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Obama Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category-inline|Wakasa-Hiko-jinja}} {{commons Category-inline|Wakasa-Hime-jinja}} *[https://www.jinja-fukui.jp/detail/index.php?ID=20151027_171130 Fukui Jinja Honcho] *[https://www.fuku-e.com/010_spot/?id=570 Fukui Tourist Association site] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} ]] ]]{{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Wakasa Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Fukui Prefecture]] [[sn:Wakasahiko Shrine]]}} [[file:101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan07s5.jpg|right|thumb|260px|Wakasahime Jinja]] [[file:Wakasa-Hime-jinja Onyu Festival 001.jpg|right|thumb|260px|Onyu Festival at Wakasahime Jinja

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shrine in Obama, Fukui}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Wakasahiko Jinja
若狭彦神社 | image = 101120 Wakasahiko-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan07s5.jpg | caption = Wakasahiko Jinja Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Fukui Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|27|57.9|N|135|46|42.5|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Hoori; Toyotama-hime | founded_by = | established = c.714 | date_destroyed = | location =Obama-shi, Fukui-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = October 10 (upper shrine)
March 10 (lower shrine) | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Wakasahiko Jinja'''|若狭彦神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Obama, Fukui|Obama]] in [[Fukui Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Wakasa Province]]. The main festivals of the shrine are held annually on October 10 and March 10.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is actually a twin shrine, consisting of the {{nihongo|'''Wakasahiko Shrine'''|若狭彦神社| Wakasahiko jinja}}, or "upper shrine", and the {{nihongo|'''Wakasahime Shrine'''|若狭姫神社| Wakasahime jinja}}, or "lower shrine". It is also sometimes referred to as the {{nihongo|'''Onyu Myōjin'''|若狭彦神社}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Wakasahiko Jinja are: * Upper shrine: {{nihongo|'''[[Hoori|Hikohohodemi no Mikoto]]'''| 彦火火出見尊|}}, the son of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] and grandfather of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] * Lower shrine: {{nihongo|'''[[Toyotama-hime]]'''| 豊玉姫命|}}, the daughter of the sea deity, [[Watatsumi]]. ==Overview== The shrine is located at the foot of Mt. Tadagadake in the southeast from the center of Obama city. Wakasahiko Shrine was formerly worshipped by seafarers, as Hoori is said to have obtained magical beads with which he could manipulate the tides while residing at [[Ryūgū-jō]]; however, today he is regarded as the god of ''[[tatami]]'' mats, and is now also worshiped by people involved in [[interior decoration]]. Wakasahime Shrine is said to have a spiritual power for easy delivery and childcare. Currently, most of the festivals are held at the lower shrine, Wakasahime Shrine, and the priesthood is also resident only at the lower shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} ==History== The origins of Wakasahiko Jinja are unknown. According to the shrine's legend, the two ''kami'' appeared in the guise of people from [[Tang dynasty|Tang]] at Shiraishi hamlet in Shimonegori village, Onyu County and the Wakasahiko Jinja was built in 714. It was related to its present location in 715. The lower shrine, Wakasahime Jinja was built in 721. The shrine first appears in historical documentation in the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' in an entry dated 859, when the upper shrine was promoted to senior second Rank, and the lower shrine to junior third Rank. In the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records of 927, the shrine is listed as a ''Myōjin Taisha''. By the [[Kamakura period]], the Wakasahiko Jinja was named the ''ichinomiya'' and the Wakasahime Jinja as the ''ninomiya'' of the province. Originally the upper shrine was the center of rituals, but this shifted to the lower shrine in the [[Muromachi period]]. During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|national shrine, 2nd Rank|国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}}, under the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. == Precincts == === Wakasahiko Jinja (Upper shrine) === * [[Honden]] – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * [[Rōmon]] (Zuishin-mon) – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Gate – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property 101120 Wakasahiko-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan12s.jpg|Rōmon 101120 Wakasahiko-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan11s.jpg|Gate 若狭彦神社陰陽石.jpg|Yin-Yang stone === Wakasahime Jinja (Lower shrine) === * Honden – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Romon (Zuishin-mon) – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Gate – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Noh Stage * Shaso – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property 101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan01s5.jpg|Romon 101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan04s5.jpg|Gate 101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan06s5.jpg|Noh stage The Wakasahiko Jinja is located a 30-minute walk and the Wakasahime Jinja a ten-minute walk from [[Higashi-Obama Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Obama Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category-inline|Wakasa-Hiko-jinja}} {{commons Category-inline|Wakasa-Hime-jinja}} *[https://www.jinja-fukui.jp/detail/index.php?ID=20151027_171130 Fukui Jinja Honcho] *[https://www.fuku-e.com/010_spot/?id=570 Fukui Tourist Association site] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} ]] ]]{{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Wakasa Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Fukui Prefecture]] [[sn:Wakasahiko Shrine]]}} [[file:101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan07s5.jpg|right|thumb|260px|Wakasahime Jinja]] [[file:Wakasa-Hime-jinja Onyu Festival 001.jpg|right|thumb|260px|Onyu Festival at Wakasahime Jinja
(Festival dedicated to God)]] [[sn:Wakasahiko Shrine]] [[en:Wakasahiko Shrine]] [[sn:akasahiko Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Wakasa-Hiko-jinja}} [[ja:若狭彦神社]] [[zh:若狹彥神社]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]]

{{Short description|Shrine in Obama, Fukui}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Wakasahiko Jinja
若狭彦神社 | image = 101120 Wakasahiko-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan07s5.jpg | caption = Wakasahiko Jinja Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Fukui Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|27|57.9|N|135|46|42.5|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Hoori; Toyotama-hime | founded_by = | established = c.714 | date_destroyed = | location =Obama-shi, Fukui-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = October 10 (upper shrine)
March 10 (lower shrine) | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Wakasahiko Jinja'''|若狭彦神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Obama, Fukui|Obama]] in [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Wakasa Province]]. The main festivals of the shrine are held annually on October 10 and March 10.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is actually a twin shrine, consisting of the {{nihongo|'''Wakasahiko Shrine'''|若狭彦神社| Wakasahiko jinja}}, or "upper shrine", and the {{nihongo|'''Wakasahime Shrine'''|若狭姫神社| Wakasahime jinja}}, or "lower shrine". It is also sometimes referred to as the {{nihongo|'''Onyu Myōjin'''|若狭彦神社}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Wakasahiko Jinja are: * Upper shrine: {{nihongo|'''[[Hoori|Hikohohodemi no Mikoto]]'''| 彦火火出見尊|}}, the son of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] and grandfather of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] * Lower shrine: {{nihongo|'''[[Toyotama-hime]]'''| 豊玉姫命|}}, the daughter of the sea deity, [[Watatsumi]]. ==Overview== The shrine is located at the foot of Mt. Tadagadake in the southeast from the center of Obama city. Wakasahiko Shrine was formerly worshipped by seafarers, as Hoori is said to have obtained magical beads with which he could manipulate the tides while residing at [[Ryūgū-jō]]; however, today he is regarded as the god of ''[[tatami]]'' mats, and is now also worshiped by people involved in [[interior decoration]]. Wakasahime Shrine is said to have a spiritual power for easy delivery and childcare. Currently, most of the festivals are held at the lower shrine, Wakasahime Shrine, and the priesthood is also resident only at the lower shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} ==History== The origins of Wakasahiko Jinja are unknown. According to the shrine's legend, the two ''kami'' appeared in the guise of people from [[Tang dynasty|Tang]] at Shiraishi hamlet in Shimonegori village, Onyu County and the Wakasahiko Jinja was built in 714. It was related to its present location in 715. The lower shrine, Wakasahime Jinja was built in 721. The shrine first appears in historical documentation in the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' in an entry dated 859, when the upper shrine was promoted to senior second Rank, and the lower shrine to junior third Rank. In the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records of 927, the shrine is listed as a ''Myōjin Taisha''. By the [[Kamakura period]], the Wakasahiko Jinja was named the ''ichinomiya'' and the Wakasahime Jinja as the ''ninomiya'' of the province. Originally the upper shrine was the center of rituals, but this shifted to the lower shrine in the [[Muromachi period]]. During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|national shrine, 2nd Rank|国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}}, under the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. == Precincts == === Wakasahiko Jinja (Upper shrine) === * [[Honden]] – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * [[Rōmon]] (Zuishin-mon) – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Gate – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property 101120 Wakasahiko-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan12s.jpg|Rōmon 101120 Wakasahiko-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan11s.jpg|Gate 若狭彦神社陰陽石.jpg|Yin-Yang stone === Wakasahime Jinja (Lower shrine) === * Honden – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Romon (Zuishin-mon) – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Gate – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Noh Stage * Shaso – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property 101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan01s5.jpg|Romon 101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan04s5.jpg|Gate 101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan06s5.jpg|Noh stage The Wakasahiko Jinja is located a 30-minute walk and the Wakasahime Jinja a ten-minute walk from [[:en:Higashi-Obama Station|Higashi-Obama Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Obama Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category-inline|Wakasa-Hiko-jinja}} {{commons Category-inline|Wakasa-Hime-jinja}} *[https://www.jinja-fukui.jp/detail/index.php?ID=20151027_171130 Fukui Jinja Honcho] *[https://www.fuku-e.com/010_spot/?id=570 Fukui Tourist Association site] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} ]] ]]{{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Wakasa Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Fukui Prefecture]] [[sn:Wakasahiko Shrine]]}} [[file:101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan07s5.jpg|right|thumb|260px|Wakasahime Jinja]] [[file:Wakasa-Hime-jinja Onyu Festival 001.jpg|right|thumb|260px|Onyu Festival at Wakasahime Jinja
(Festival dedicated to God)]] [[sn:Wakasahiko Shrine]] [[en:Wakasahiko Shrine]] [[sn:akasahiko Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Wakasa-Hiko-jinja}} [[ja:若狭彦神社]] [[zh:若狹彥神社]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]]

During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|national shrine, 2nd Rank|国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}}, under the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. == Precincts == === Wakasahiko Jinja (Upper shrine) === * [[Honden]] – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * [[Rōmon]] (Zuishin-mon) – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Gate – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property 101120 Wakasahiko-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan12s.jpg|Rōmon 101120 Wakasahiko-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan11s.jpg|Gate 若狭彦神社陰陽石.jpg|Yin-Yang stone === Wakasahime Jinja (Lower shrine) === * Honden – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Romon (Zuishin-mon) – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Gate – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Noh Stage * Shaso – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property 101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan01s5.jpg|Romon 101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan04s5.jpg|Gate 101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan06s5.jpg|Noh stage The Wakasahiko Jinja is located a 30-minute walk and the Wakasahime Jinja a ten-minute walk from [[:en:Higashi-Obama Station|Higashi-Obama Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[Obama Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). {{Short description|Shrine in Obama, Fukui}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Wakasahiko Jinja
若狭彦神社 | image = 101120 Wakasahiko-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan07s5.jpg | caption = Wakasahiko Jinja Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Fukui Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|27|57.9|N|135|46|42.5|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Hoori; Toyotama-hime | founded_by = | established = c.714 | date_destroyed = | location =Obama-shi, Fukui-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = October 10 (upper shrine)
March 10 (lower shrine) | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Wakasahiko Jinja'''|若狭彦神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Obama, Fukui|Obama]] in [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Wakasa Province]]. The main festivals of the shrine are held annually on October 10 and March 10.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is actually a twin shrine, consisting of the {{nihongo|'''Wakasahiko Shrine'''|若狭彦神社| Wakasahiko jinja}}, or "upper shrine", and the {{nihongo|'''Wakasahime Shrine'''|若狭姫神社| Wakasahime jinja}}, or "lower shrine". It is also sometimes referred to as the {{nihongo|'''Onyu Myōjin'''|若狭彦神社}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Wakasahiko Jinja are: * Upper shrine: {{nihongo|'''[[Hoori|Hikohohodemi no Mikoto]]'''| 彦火火出見尊|}}, the son of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] and grandfather of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] * Lower shrine: {{nihongo|'''[[Toyotama-hime]]'''| 豊玉姫命|}}, the daughter of the sea deity, [[Watatsumi]]. ==Overview== The shrine is located at the foot of Mt. Tadagadake in the southeast from the center of Obama city. Wakasahiko Shrine was formerly worshipped by seafarers, as Hoori is said to have obtained magical beads with which he could manipulate the tides while residing at [[Ryūgū-jō]]; however, today he is regarded as the god of ''[[tatami]]'' mats, and is now also worshiped by people involved in [[:en:Interior decoration|Interior decoration]]. Wakasahime Shrine is said to have a spiritual power for easy delivery and childcare. Currently, most of the festivals are held at the lower shrine, Wakasahime Shrine, and the priesthood is also resident only at the lower shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} ==History== The origins of Wakasahiko Jinja are unknown. According to the shrine's legend, the two ''kami'' appeared in the guise of people from [[Tang dynasty|Tang]] at Shiraishi hamlet in Shimonegori village, Onyu County and the Wakasahiko Jinja was built in 714. It was related to its present location in 715. The lower shrine, Wakasahime Jinja was built in 721. The shrine first appears in historical documentation in the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' in an entry dated 859, when the upper shrine was promoted to senior second Rank, and the lower shrine to junior third Rank. In the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records of 927, the shrine is listed as a ''Myōjin Taisha''. By the [[Kamakura period]], the Wakasahiko Jinja was named the ''ichinomiya'' and the Wakasahime Jinja as the ''ninomiya'' of the province. Originally the upper shrine was the center of rituals, but this shifted to the lower shrine in the [[Muromachi period]]. During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|national shrine, 2nd Rank|国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}}, under the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. == Precincts == === Wakasahiko Jinja (Upper shrine) === * [[Honden]] – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * [[Rōmon]] (Zuishin-mon) – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Gate – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property 101120 Wakasahiko-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan12s.jpg|Rōmon 101120 Wakasahiko-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan11s.jpg|Gate 若狭彦神社陰陽石.jpg|Yin-Yang stone === Wakasahime Jinja (Lower shrine) === * Honden – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Romon (Zuishin-mon) – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Gate – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Noh Stage * Shaso – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property 101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan01s5.jpg|Romon 101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan04s5.jpg|Gate 101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan06s5.jpg|Noh stage The Wakasahiko Jinja is located a 30-minute walk and the Wakasahime Jinja a ten-minute walk from [[:en:Higashi-Obama Station|Higashi-Obama Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[Obama Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category-inline|Wakasa-Hiko-jinja}} {{commons Category-inline|Wakasa-Hime-jinja}} *[https://www.jinja-fukui.jp/detail/index.php?ID=20151027_171130 Fukui Jinja Honcho] *[https://www.fuku-e.com/010_spot/?id=570 Fukui Tourist Association site] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} ]] ]]{{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Wakasa Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Fukui Prefecture]] [[sn:Wakasahiko Shrine]]}} [[file:101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan07s5.jpg|right|thumb|260px|Wakasahime Jinja]] [[file:Wakasa-Hime-jinja Onyu Festival 001.jpg|right|thumb|260px|Onyu Festival at Wakasahime Jinja
(Festival dedicated to God)]] [[sn:Wakasahiko Shrine]] [[en:Wakasahiko Shrine]] [[sn:akasahiko Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Wakasa-Hiko-jinja}} [[ja:若狭彦神社]] [[zh:若狹彥神社]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]]

(Festival dedicated to God)]] [[sn:Wakasahiko Shrine]] [[en:Wakasahiko Shrine]] [[sn:akasahiko Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Wakasa-Hiko-jinja}} [[ja:若狭彦神社]] [[zh:若狹彥神社]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shrine in Obama, Fukui}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Wakasahiko Jinja
若狭彦神社 | image = 101120 Wakasahiko-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan07s5.jpg | caption = Wakasahiko Jinja Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Fukui Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|27|57.9|N|135|46|42.5|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Hoori; Toyotama-hime | founded_by = | established = c.714 | date_destroyed = | location =Obama-shi, Fukui-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = October 10 (upper shrine)
March 10 (lower shrine) | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Wakasahiko Jinja'''|若狭彦神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Obama, Fukui|Obama]] in [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Wakasa Province]]. The main festivals of the shrine are held annually on October 10 and March 10.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is actually a twin shrine, consisting of the {{nihongo|'''Wakasahiko Shrine'''|若狭彦神社| Wakasahiko jinja}}, or "upper shrine", and the {{nihongo|'''Wakasahime Shrine'''|若狭姫神社| Wakasahime jinja}}, or "lower shrine". It is also sometimes referred to as the {{nihongo|'''Onyu Myōjin'''|若狭彦神社}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Wakasahiko Jinja are: * Upper shrine: {{nihongo|'''[[Hoori|Hikohohodemi no Mikoto]]'''| 彦火火出見尊|}}, the son of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] and grandfather of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] * Lower shrine: {{nihongo|'''[[Toyotama-hime]]'''| 豊玉姫命|}}, the daughter of the sea deity, [[Watatsumi]]. ==Overview== The shrine is located at the foot of Mt. Tadagadake in the southeast from the center of Obama city. Wakasahiko Shrine was formerly worshipped by seafarers, as Hoori is said to have obtained magical beads with which he could manipulate the tides while residing at [[Ryūgū-jō]]; however, today he is regarded as the god of ''[[tatami]]'' mats, and is now also worshiped by people involved in [[:en:Interior decoration|Interior decoration]]. Wakasahime Shrine is said to have a spiritual power for easy delivery and childcare. Currently, most of the festivals are held at the lower shrine, Wakasahime Shrine, and the priesthood is also resident only at the lower shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} ==History== The origins of Wakasahiko Jinja are unknown. According to the shrine's legend, the two ''kami'' appeared in the guise of people from [[Tang dynasty|Tang]] at Shiraishi hamlet in Shimonegori village, Onyu County and the Wakasahiko Jinja was built in 714. It was related to its present location in 715. The lower shrine, Wakasahime Jinja was built in 721. The shrine first appears in historical documentation in the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' in an entry dated 859, when the upper shrine was promoted to senior second Rank, and the lower shrine to junior third Rank. In the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records of 927, the shrine is listed as a ''Myōjin Taisha''. By the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the Wakasahiko Jinja was named the ''ichinomiya'' and the Wakasahime Jinja as the ''ninomiya'' of the province. Originally the upper shrine was the center of rituals, but this shifted to the lower shrine in the [[Muromachi period]]. During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|national shrine, 2nd Rank|国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}}, under the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. == Precincts == === Wakasahiko Jinja (Upper shrine) === * [[Honden]] – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * [[Rōmon]] (Zuishin-mon) – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Gate – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property 101120 Wakasahiko-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan12s.jpg|Rōmon 101120 Wakasahiko-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan11s.jpg|Gate 若狭彦神社陰陽石.jpg|Yin-Yang stone === Wakasahime Jinja (Lower shrine) === * Honden – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Romon (Zuishin-mon) – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Gate – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Noh Stage * Shaso – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property 101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan01s5.jpg|Romon 101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan04s5.jpg|Gate 101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan06s5.jpg|Noh stage The Wakasahiko Jinja is located a 30-minute walk and the Wakasahime Jinja a ten-minute walk from [[:en:Higashi-Obama Station|Higashi-Obama Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[Obama Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category-inline|Wakasa-Hiko-jinja}} {{commons Category-inline|Wakasa-Hime-jinja}} *[https://www.jinja-fukui.jp/detail/index.php?ID=20151027_171130 Fukui Jinja Honcho] *[https://www.fuku-e.com/010_spot/?id=570 Fukui Tourist Association site] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} ]] ]]{{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Wakasa Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Fukui Prefecture]] [[sn:Wakasahiko Shrine]]}} [[file:101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan07s5.jpg|right|thumb|260px|Wakasahime Jinja]] [[file:Wakasa-Hime-jinja Onyu Festival 001.jpg|right|thumb|260px|Onyu Festival at Wakasahime Jinja
(Festival dedicated to God)]] [[sn:Wakasahiko Shrine]] [[en:Wakasahiko Shrine]] [[sn:akasahiko Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Wakasa-Hiko-jinja}} [[ja:若狭彦神社]] [[zh:若狹彥神社]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]]

It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Wakasa Province]]. The main festivals of the shrine are held annually on October 10 and March 10.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is actually a twin shrine, consisting of the {{nihongo|'''Wakasahiko Shrine'''|若狭彦神社| Wakasahiko jinja}}, or "upper shrine", and the {{nihongo|'''Wakasahime Shrine'''|若狭姫神社| Wakasahime jinja}}, or "lower shrine". It is also sometimes referred to as the {{nihongo|'''Onyu Myōjin'''|若狭彦神社}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Wakasahiko Jinja are: * Upper shrine: {{nihongo|'''[[Hoori|Hikohohodemi no Mikoto]]'''| 彦火火出見尊|}}, the son of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] and grandfather of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] * Lower shrine: {{nihongo|'''[[Toyotama-hime]]'''| 豊玉姫命|}}, the daughter of the sea deity, [[Watatsumi]]. ==Overview== The shrine is located at the foot of Mt. Tadagadake in the southeast from the center of Obama city. Wakasahiko Shrine was formerly worshipped by seafarers, as Hoori is said to have obtained magical beads with which he could manipulate the tides while residing at [[Ryūgū-jō]]; however, today he is regarded as the god of ''[[tatami]]'' mats, and is now also worshiped by people involved in [[:en:Interior decoration|Interior decoration]]. Wakasahime Shrine is said to have a spiritual power for easy delivery and childcare. Currently, most of the festivals are held at the lower shrine, Wakasahime Shrine, and the priesthood is also resident only at the lower shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} ==History== The origins of Wakasahiko Jinja are unknown. According to the shrine's legend, the two ''kami'' appeared in the guise of people from [[Tang dynasty|Tang]] at Shiraishi hamlet in Shimonegori village, Onyu County and the Wakasahiko Jinja was built in 714. It was related to its present location in 715. The lower shrine, Wakasahime Jinja was built in 721. The shrine first appears in historical documentation in the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' in an entry dated 859, when the upper shrine was promoted to senior second Rank, and the lower shrine to junior third Rank. In the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records of 927, the shrine is listed as a ''Myōjin Taisha''. By the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the Wakasahiko Jinja was named the ''ichinomiya'' and the Wakasahime Jinja as the ''ninomiya'' of the province. Originally the upper shrine was the center of rituals, but this shifted to the lower shrine in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] era of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|national shrine, 2nd Rank|国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}}, under the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. == Precincts == === Wakasahiko Jinja (Upper shrine) === * [[Honden]] – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * [[Rōmon]] (Zuishin-mon) – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Gate – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property 101120 Wakasahiko-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan12s.jpg|Rōmon 101120 Wakasahiko-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan11s.jpg|Gate 若狭彦神社陰陽石.jpg|Yin-Yang stone === Wakasahime Jinja (Lower shrine) === * Honden – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Romon (Zuishin-mon) – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Gate – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Noh Stage * Shaso – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property 101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan01s5.jpg|Romon 101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan04s5.jpg|Gate 101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan06s5.jpg|Noh stage The Wakasahiko Jinja is located a 30-minute walk and the Wakasahime Jinja a ten-minute walk from [[:en:Higashi-Obama Station|Higashi-Obama Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[Obama Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category-inline|Wakasa-Hiko-jinja}} {{commons Category-inline|Wakasa-Hime-jinja}} *[https://www.jinja-fukui.jp/detail/index.php?ID=20151027_171130 Fukui Jinja Honcho] *[https://www.fuku-e.com/010_spot/?id=570 Fukui Tourist Association site] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} ]] ]]{{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Wakasa Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Fukui Prefecture]] [[sn:Wakasahiko Shrine]]}} [[file:101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan07s5.jpg|right|thumb|260px|Wakasahime Jinja]] [[file:Wakasa-Hime-jinja Onyu Festival 001.jpg|right|thumb|260px|Onyu Festival at Wakasahime Jinja
(Festival dedicated to God)]] [[sn:Wakasahiko Shrine]] [[en:Wakasahiko Shrine]] [[sn:akasahiko Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Wakasa-Hiko-jinja}} [[ja:若狭彦神社]] [[zh:若狹彥神社]]

''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. == Precincts == === Wakasahiko Jinja (Upper shrine) === * [[Honden]] – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * [[Rōmon]] (Zuishin-mon) – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Gate – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property 101120 Wakasahiko-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan12s.jpg|Rōmon 101120 Wakasahiko-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan11s.jpg|Gate 若狭彦神社陰陽石.jpg|Yin-Yang stone === Wakasahime Jinja (Lower shrine) === * Honden – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Romon (Zuishin-mon) – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Gate – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Noh Stage * Shaso – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property 101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan01s5.jpg|Romon 101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan04s5.jpg|Gate 101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan06s5.jpg|Noh stage The Wakasahiko Jinja is located a 30-minute walk and the Wakasahime Jinja a ten-minute walk from [[:en:Higashi-Obama Station|Higashi-Obama Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Obama Line|Obama Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category-inline|Wakasa-Hiko-jinja}} {{commons Category-inline|Wakasa-Hime-jinja}} *[https://www.jinja-fukui.jp/detail/index.php?ID=20151027_171130 Fukui Jinja Honcho] *[https://www.fuku-e.com/010_spot/?id=570 Fukui Tourist Association site] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} ]] ]]{{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Wakasa Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Fukui Prefecture]] [[sn:Wakasahiko Shrine]]}} [[file:101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan07s5.jpg|right|thumb|260px|Wakasahime Jinja]] [[file:Wakasa-Hime-jinja Onyu Festival 001.jpg|right|thumb|260px|Onyu Festival at Wakasahime Jinja

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shrine in Obama, Fukui}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Wakasahiko Jinja
若狭彦神社 | image = 101120 Wakasahiko-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan07s5.jpg | caption = Wakasahiko Jinja Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Fukui Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|27|57.9|N|135|46|42.5|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Hoori; Toyotama-hime | founded_by = | established = c.714 | date_destroyed = | location =Obama-shi, Fukui-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival = October 10 (upper shrine)
March 10 (lower shrine) | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Wakasahiko Jinja'''|若狭彦神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[:en:Obama, Fukui|Obama]] in [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Wakasa Province|Wakasa Province]]. The main festivals of the shrine are held annually on October 10 and March 10.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is actually a twin shrine, consisting of the {{nihongo|'''Wakasahiko Shrine'''|若狭彦神社| Wakasahiko jinja}}, or "upper shrine", and the {{nihongo|'''Wakasahime Shrine'''|若狭姫神社| Wakasahime jinja}}, or "lower shrine". It is also sometimes referred to as the {{nihongo|'''Onyu Myōjin'''|若狭彦神社}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Wakasahiko Jinja are: * Upper shrine: {{nihongo|'''[[Hoori|Hikohohodemi no Mikoto]]'''| 彦火火出見尊|}}, the son of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] and grandfather of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] * Lower shrine: {{nihongo|'''[[Toyotama-hime]]'''| 豊玉姫命|}}, the daughter of the sea deity, [[:en:Watatsumi|Watatsumi]]. ==Overview== The shrine is located at the foot of Mt. Tadagadake in the southeast from the center of Obama city. Wakasahiko Shrine was formerly worshipped by seafarers, as Hoori is said to have obtained magical beads with which he could manipulate the tides while residing at [[Ryūgū-jō]]; however, today he is regarded as the god of ''[[tatami]]'' mats, and is now also worshiped by people involved in [[:en:Interior decoration|Interior decoration]]. Wakasahime Shrine is said to have a spiritual power for easy delivery and childcare. Currently, most of the festivals are held at the lower shrine, Wakasahime Shrine, and the priesthood is also resident only at the lower shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} ==History== The origins of Wakasahiko Jinja are unknown. According to the shrine's legend, the two ''kami'' appeared in the guise of people from [[Tang dynasty|Tang]] at Shiraishi hamlet in Shimonegori village, Onyu County and the Wakasahiko Jinja was built in 714. It was related to its present location in 715. The lower shrine, Wakasahime Jinja was built in 721. The shrine first appears in historical documentation in the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' in an entry dated 859, when the upper shrine was promoted to senior second Rank, and the lower shrine to junior third Rank. In the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records of 927, the shrine is listed as a ''Myōjin Taisha''. By the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the Wakasahiko Jinja was named the ''ichinomiya'' and the Wakasahime Jinja as the ''ninomiya'' of the province. Originally the upper shrine was the center of rituals, but this shifted to the lower shrine in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] era of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|national shrine, 2nd Rank|国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}}, under the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. == Precincts == === Wakasahiko Jinja (Upper shrine) === * [[Honden]] – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * [[Rōmon]] (Zuishin-mon) – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Gate – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property 101120 Wakasahiko-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan12s.jpg|Rōmon 101120 Wakasahiko-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan11s.jpg|Gate 若狭彦神社陰陽石.jpg|Yin-Yang stone === Wakasahime Jinja (Lower shrine) === * Honden – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Romon (Zuishin-mon) – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Gate – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property * Noh Stage * Shaso – Fukui Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property 101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan01s5.jpg|Romon 101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan04s5.jpg|Gate 101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan06s5.jpg|Noh stage The Wakasahiko Jinja is located a 30-minute walk and the Wakasahime Jinja a ten-minute walk from [[:en:Higashi-Obama Station|Higashi-Obama Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Obama Line|Obama Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category-inline|Wakasa-Hiko-jinja}} {{commons Category-inline|Wakasa-Hime-jinja}} *[https://www.jinja-fukui.jp/detail/index.php?ID=20151027_171130 Fukui Jinja Honcho] *[https://www.fuku-e.com/010_spot/?id=570 Fukui Tourist Association site] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} ]] ]]{{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Wakasa Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Fukui Prefecture]] [[sn:Wakasahiko Shrine]]}} [[file:101120 Wakasahime-jinja Obama Fukui pref Japan07s5.jpg|right|thumb|260px|Wakasahime Jinja]] [[file:Wakasa-Hime-jinja Onyu Festival 001.jpg|right|thumb|260px|Onyu Festival at Wakasahime Jinja
(Festival dedicated to God)]] [[sn:Wakasahiko Shrine]] [[en:Wakasahiko Shrine]] [[sn:akasahiko Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Wakasa-Hiko-jinja}} [[ja:若狭彦神社]] [[zh:若狹彥神社]]

(Festival dedicated to God)]] [[sn:Wakasahiko Shrine]] [[en:Wakasahiko Shrine]] [[sn:akasahiko Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Wakasa-Hiko-jinja}} [[ja:若狭彦神社]] [[zh:若狹彥神社]]
